heroOverview: A rectocele forms when the fibromuscular wall separating the rectum from the vagina weakens or tears, allowing the front wall of the rectum to bulge forward into the vaginal canal. The condition affects an estimated 20–40% of women who have delivered vaginally and is the most common cause of a posterior vaginal bulge. Most rectoceles remain small and asymptomatic, but moderate-to-large defects cause a visible or palpable vaginal lump, constipation, incomplete bowel emptying, and the need to splint the vaginal wall with a finger to complete defecation. Pelvic floor physical therapy reduces symptoms in mild cases; surgical repair is reserved for women whose quality of life is significantly impaired and who have completed childbearing.

keyStats.prevalence: Symptomatic posterior vaginal wall prolapse affects approximately 20–40% of parous women; found incidentally on examination in up to 76% of women having routine pelvic exams
keyStats.demographics: Almost exclusively affects women; peak incidence in the sixth and seventh decades of life
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed between ages 50 and 70, though onset can follow vaginal delivery at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Pelvic organ prolapse overall affects an estimated 50% of parous women globally, with rectocele representing the most common posterior compartment defect

definition: A rectocele (ICD-10: N81.6) is a herniation of the posterior vaginal wall in which the rectovaginal fascia — the connective-tissue septum lying between the rectum and the vagina — becomes attenuated or disrupted, permitting the rectal ampulla to protrude anteriorly into the vaginal lumen. The defect is classified by severity: Grade 1 (small, <2 cm protrusion into the vaginal lumen), Grade 2 (moderate, reaching the vaginal introitus), and Grade 3 (large, extending beyond the introitus). Symptomatic rectoceles fall under the broader diagnosis of posterior compartment pelvic organ prolapse and are managed by urogynecologists, gynecologists, or colorectal surgeons with pelvic floor expertise. The condition nearly always coexists with some degree of perineal body weakness and frequently accompanies anterior compartment defects such as cystocele or central compartment defects such as uterine prolapse. The pelvic organ prolapse quantification (POP-Q) system is the internationally accepted standard grading tool used in clinical practice and research worldwide.

typesClassification.description: The posterior vaginal wall protrudes less than 2 cm into the vaginal canal. Usually asymptomatic and found incidentally; conservative management is appropriate.
The bulge reaches the vaginal introitus. Women often notice a vaginal heaviness or lump and may have difficulty evacuating stool.
The posterior wall protrudes beyond the vaginal opening and may be visible externally. Splinting, significant constipation, and pelvic pressure are common at this stage.
A defect near the top of the posterior vaginal wall, often associated with enterocele or apical prolapse, requiring repair techniques that address the upper compartment.

primarySymptoms: A bulge or lump at or beyond the vaginal opening — often described as a feeling that something is 'falling out' of the vagina, which is the hallmark complaint in moderate-to-severe rectocele.
Incomplete bowel emptying — the sensation that stool remains after a bowel movement, caused by the rectal wall herniating into the vaginal space and creating a pocket where stool collects.

Need to manually splint or digitally reduce — pressing upward on the posterior vaginal wall or perineum with a finger to straighten the rectum and allow complete defecation; reported by 30–75% of women with large rectoceles.
Pelvic pressure or heaviness, typically worsening throughout the day and with prolonged standing, straining, or lifting, as gravity draws the prolapse further downward.
Straining at stool — excessive effort needed to initiate or complete defecation, often accompanied by prolonged time on the toilet.
Constipation or infrequent stools, a consequence of the functional obstruction created by the herniated rectal pouch.
Dyspareunia — pain or discomfort during vaginal intercourse, arising from mechanical distortion of the posterior vaginal wall.
Vaginal or perineal fullness that eases when lying down, consistent with the gravity-dependent nature of prolapse.
Fecal urgency or incontinence in women with concurrent perineal body disruption or internal anal sphincter weakness.
Rectal pressure or discomfort that patients often misattribute to hemorrhoids before the underlying prolapse is identified.
earlyWarningSigns: A mild feeling of vaginal heaviness that appears after long periods of standing or at the end of the day.
Straining more than usual to complete a bowel movement despite a soft stool.
A subtle sense that the bowel has not fully emptied after defecation.
Noticing a small soft bulge at the vaginal opening when wiping after using the toilet.
emergencySigns: Complete procidentia — the entire vaginal wall protrudes beyond the body; the exposed mucosa can ulcerate and bleed.
Inability to urinate or defecate because of a large, incarcerated prolapse blocking the bladder outlet or rectum.
Signs of mucosal ulceration: bleeding, discharge, or a raw area on the visible vaginal tissue.
Severe lower pelvic or rectal pain suggesting strangulation of prolapsed tissue — rare but requires urgent surgical assessment.

causes.description: Stretching and tearing of the rectovaginal fascia and perineal body during vaginal delivery is the most common inciting event. Instrumental delivery (forceps or vacuum), prolonged second-stage labour, and large infants increase fascial disruption; histological tears are demonstrable in up to 80% of primiparous women after vaginal delivery.

The pelvic fascia and ligaments lose collagen content and tensile strength after the fifth decade. This age-related attenuation is compounded by oestrogen decline at menopause, which further reduces collagen synthesis and tissue elasticity in the rectovaginal septum and perineal body.

Repeated Valsalva manoeuvre during defecation generates prolonged downward pressure on the posterior vaginal wall. Over years, this mechanical stress progressively stretches and weakens the rectovaginal fascia, contributing to or worsening rectocele formation.

Oestrogen promotes collagen synthesis in pelvic connective tissue. Its decline after natural or surgical menopause accelerates fascial thinning, making postmenopausal women substantially more susceptible to prolapse progression even without additional obstetric injury.

Chronic conditions that raise intra-abdominal pressure — obesity, a persistent chronic cough, heavy occupational lifting, or severe constipation — exert sustained downward force on the pelvic floor, hastening the descent of a previously subclinical defect.

Hysterectomy removes the uterosacral and cardinal ligaments that anchor the vaginal apex, reducing the structural scaffold for the posterior wall. Both abdominal and vaginal hysterectomy are associated with a subsequent increase in posterior compartment prolapse within five to ten years.

riskFactors.description: Each vaginal delivery approximately doubles the risk of clinically significant prolapse. Women who have had three or more vaginal deliveries carry a substantially higher lifetime risk than nulliparous women.
Excess body weight increases chronic intra-abdominal pressure, placing continuous strain on pelvic support structures. Weight loss measurably reduces prolapse symptoms and the risk of progression.
Habitual straining to defecate is an independent risk factor; addressing constipation with fibre, fluids, and stool softeners reduces mechanical stress on the rectovaginal fascia.

Natural or surgical menopause without hormone therapy accelerates pelvic connective-tissue atrophy; topical vaginal oestrogen can partially restore tissue integrity but does not reverse established prolapse.
Removal of the uterus eliminates the primary apical support of the vaginal vault; posterior wall descent is seen in 3–12% of women within five years of hysterectomy.

Heritable variation in collagen subtypes (particularly types I and III) and connective-tissue disorders such as Marfan syndrome or Ehlers–Danlos syndrome substantially elevate risk; first-degree relatives of affected women have roughly twice the prevalence.

riskFactors.factor: Heavy physical labour or chronic lifting
riskFactors.description: Occupations requiring repeated heavy lifting or high-impact exercise increase the cumulative load on pelvic floor structures; ergonomic adjustments and pelvic floor strengthening are protective.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease or asthma
riskFactors.description: Chronic cough generates repeated spikes in intra-abdominal pressure. Optimising respiratory control reduces prolapse progression in affected women.

affectedDemographics: Parous women of any age, particularly those with multiple vaginal deliveries
Postmenopausal women, in whom oestrogen deficiency accelerates fascial atrophy
Women aged 50–70 presenting with worsening pelvic floor symptoms
Women with a prior history of hysterectomy
Women with chronic constipation or a history of straining at stool
Women with connective-tissue disorders such as Ehlers–Danlos syndrome or Marfan syndrome
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is primarily clinical, made by examining the posterior vaginal wall at rest and during a Valsalva manoeuvre or cough. With the patient supine and later standing, the clinician uses a Sims speculum or the posterior blade of a bivalve speculum to isolate and grade the posterior wall descent using the POP-Q system, which records the position of defined vaginal points relative to the hymenal ring in centimetres. Grade 2 or higher prolapse that reproduces the patient's symptoms on examination confirms symptomatic rectocele. Defaecography (either conventional barium or MRI) is reserved for cases where functional obstruction is suspected but the degree of rectocele does not fully explain the symptoms, or where a concurrent enterocele or rectal intussusception needs to be distinguished. MRI of the pelvic floor provides detailed soft-tissue mapping of the levator ani, perineal body, and sphincter complex and is used in surgical planning for complex or recurrent cases. Anorectal manometry and pudendal nerve terminal motor latency testing are added when concurrent fecal incontinence or suspected sphincter disruption is present. A thorough assessment also evaluates anterior and apical compartment prolapse, urinary symptoms, and bowel function to plan comprehensive pelvic floor rehabilitation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades the extent of posterior wall descent and identifies co-existing anterior or apical prolapse, guiding management decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie on the examination table, first flat and then standing. The clinician inserts a speculum and asks you to bear down or cough while observing the vaginal walls. It takes about five minutes and causes mild discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises rectal emptying in real time, quantifies rectocele depth, and identifies concurrent conditions such as enterocele, rectal intussusception, or anismus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: For barium defaecography, a contrast paste is inserted into the rectum; you then sit on a commode and evacuate while X-ray images are taken. MRI defaecography uses a magnet rather than X-rays and gives better soft-tissue detail.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides comprehensive mapping of all three pelvic compartments, levator ani integrity, and apical support, used in surgical planning for complex or recurrent prolapse.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in an MRI scanner for 30–45 minutes. The radiologist asks you to perform Valsalva manoeuvres at intervals. No radiation is involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures anal sphincter pressures and rectal sensation to evaluate co-existing fecal incontinence or dyssynergia when these symptoms accompany the prolapse.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible probe with a small balloon is gently inserted into the rectum. Pressure readings are taken at rest and during voluntary squeeze; the test takes 20–30 minutes and is mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the anal sphincter complex and levator ani for occult obstetric tears, particularly when fecal incontinence co-exists.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small ultrasound probe is placed gently at the perineum or just inside the anus for 10–15 minutes. Results are immediately available.

treatmentOverview: Management is guided by symptom severity, prolapse grade, bowel function, and the patient's preference regarding surgery. The majority of women with Grade 1–2 rectocele benefit from conservative measures before surgery is considered. Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT), directed by a specialist physiotherapist, reduces prolapse symptoms and improves quality of life in randomised trials, with clinically meaningful benefit seen after 12–16 weeks of consistent practice. Bowel habit modification — dietary fibre intake of 25–30 g daily, adequate hydration, and avoidance of straining — reduces the mechanical load on the posterior wall and is a cornerstone of non-surgical care. A vaginal pessary can mechanically support the posterior wall and provide symptomatic relief for women who cannot or prefer not to have surgery, or as a bridge to surgical repair. Topical vaginal oestrogen is recommended for postmenopausal women before and after repair, since well-oestrogenised tissue heals better and recurrence rates are lower. Surgical repair is offered when Grade 2–3 rectocele produces significant bowel symptoms, quality-of-life impairment, or has failed at least 3–6 months of conservative treatment. Posterior colporrhaphy — midline plication of the rectovaginal fascia — is the standard surgical approach and achieves anatomical success rates of 76–96%, though its impact on bowel symptoms is more variable than its effect on the bulge.

medicalTreatments.name: Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT)
medicalTreatments.description: A structured programme of pelvic floor contractions supervised by a physiotherapist specialising in urogynaecology. Strengthening the levator ani and perineal body reduces prolapse descent and improves bowel and bladder symptoms. Programmes typically run 12–24 weeks with home practice of 3 sets of 8–12 contractions daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials show a 30–40% reduction in prolapse symptom severity and measurable reduction in POP-Q stage in approximately 40% of women after 16 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical vaginal oestrogen
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose oestrogen cream or pessary applied to the vaginal mucosa restores tissue thickness, increases collagen synthesis, and reduces mucosal atrophy in postmenopausal women. It does not reverse prolapse anatomically but improves tissue quality for conservative and surgical management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces vaginal mucosal symptoms and atrophy in over 80% of postmenopausal women; associated with a roughly 30% lower recurrence rate after surgical repair in observational studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Bowel management programme
medicalTreatments.description: Dietary modification to achieve fibre intake of 25–30 g daily, fluid intake of at least 1.5–2 litres, and a regular toileting routine. Osmotic or stimulant laxatives may be added short-term. Biofeedback therapy can address paradoxical puborectalis contraction (anismus) that worsens functional obstruction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in approximately 50–60% of women with mild-to-moderate rectocele when bowel habit is consistently optimised.

medicalTreatments.name: Vaginal pessary (ring or Gellhorn type)
medicalTreatments.description: A silicone device inserted into the vagina to mechanically support the posterior wall and reduce prolapse descent. Pessaries are fitted in the clinic, require cleaning every 3–6 months, and are suitable for women who prefer to avoid surgery or have significant medical comorbidities.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides symptomatic relief in 50–70% of women who retain the pessary long-term; roughly 50% discontinue within one year owing to discomfort or difficulty with self-management.

surgicalOptions.name: Posterior colporrhaphy
surgicalOptions.description: The standard surgical repair performed vaginally: the posterior vaginal wall is opened, the rectovaginal fascia is identified and plicated in the midline with absorbable sutures, and the vaginal mucosa is closed. It corrects the anatomical bulge and is combined with a perineorrhaphy to rebuild the perineal body when indicated.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Anatomical success rate of 76–96% at one year; subjective cure of bowel-evacuation symptoms in approximately 60–75% of women.

surgicalOptions.name: Site-specific fascial repair
surgicalOptions.description: Targeted repair of discrete fascial defects rather than midline plication, based on defect mapping from examination or MRI. Some surgeons prefer this technique for younger women to preserve normal vaginal calibre and reduce dyspareunia risk.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Anatomical recurrence rates of 8–20% at two years; comparable to midline plication in most series.

surgicalOptions.name: Transanal repair (STARR or stapled transanal rectal resection)
surgicalOptions.description: A colorectal surgical approach that excises redundant rectal mucosa and muscle through the anus using a circular stapler. Preferred when the primary complaint is obstructed defecation and radiological defaecography confirms significant rectal prolapse component rather than isolated fascial defect.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improvement in obstructed defecation in 70–90% at one year; risk of de novo fecal urgency in approximately 20% of patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Biofeedback therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Sensor-guided retraining of defecatory coordination, particularly useful when anismus (paradoxical sphincter contraction) contributes to obstructed defecation. Typically 4–8 sessions with a pelvic floor physiotherapist.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Some women report pelvic pressure relief with acupuncture; evidence is limited to small observational studies and it is not guideline-recommended as a prolapse treatment, but it may complement other pelvic floor rehabilitation for symptom management.

whySeeSpecialist: A urogynecologist or female pelvic medicine and reconstructive surgery (FPMRS) specialist should be consulted when Grade 2 or higher rectocele causes bowel symptoms that impair quality of life, when splinting is required to defecate, when conservative measures over 3–6 months have failed, or when a pessary cannot be retained. Colorectal surgical input is valuable when obstructed defecation dominates the picture and defaecography suggests a rectal rather than purely vaginal defect. Combined multi-disciplinary assessment reduces the risk of overlooking concurrent prolapse, incontinence, or sphincter injury.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a urogynecologist or FPMRS specialist with fellowship training and experience in at least 50 posterior-compartment repairs annually. Confirm access to defaecography and dynamic pelvic floor MRI for complex cases. Ask about the surgeon's recurrence rates and approach to concurrent anterior or apical prolapse, since isolated posterior repair without addressing other compartments increases the risk of new prolapse in those areas. For women with significant bowel dysfunction, a joint assessment with a colorectal surgeon experienced in pelvic floor disorders is ideal.
preventionStrategies: Maintain bowel regularity with a high-fibre diet and adequate hydration to avoid chronic straining.
Practise pelvic floor exercises starting in pregnancy and continuing postpartum to maintain levator ani strength.
Achieve or maintain a healthy weight to reduce chronic intra-abdominal pressure.
Use topical vaginal oestrogen after menopause to preserve connective-tissue integrity.
Avoid heavy lifting with poor core technique; exhale and engage the pelvic floor before any heavy lift.
Treat chronic cough or asthma aggressively to reduce repetitive pressure spikes on the pelvic floor.
lifestyleModifications: Aim for a diet providing 25–30 g of fibre daily from vegetables, whole grains, legumes, and fruit.
Drink at least 1.5–2 litres of fluid daily to keep stools soft and reduce straining.
Establish a consistent, unhurried toileting routine, ideally 20–30 minutes after a meal when the gastrocolic reflex is active.
Use a footstool to raise the feet while on the toilet, adopting a squatting-like position that straightens the anorectal angle and reduces straining force.
If digital splinting is currently necessary, continue without shame while pursuing treatment — it is a recognised self-management technique.
Avoid prolonged standing, heavy lifting, and high-impact exercise when symptoms are severe; modify gradually as pelvic floor strength improves.

recommended: High-fibre foods: oats, lentils, chickpeas, broccoli, pears, and whole-grain bread to normalise bowel transit
Adequate water and non-caffeinated fluids to soften stools
Probiotic foods such as live yoghurt and fermented vegetables to support gut microbiome and bowel regularity
Psyllium husk or methylcellulose supplements when dietary fibre is insufficient
avoid: Low-fibre processed foods, white bread, and cheese that contribute to hard stools and straining
Excessive caffeine and alcohol, which dehydrate and can worsen constipation
Large meals in the evening when bowel activity is lower, which may worsen morning straining

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact exercise such as walking, swimming, and cycling is encouraged and does not worsen rectocele when combined with proper pelvic floor engagement. High-impact activities — running, jumping, or heavy weightlifting — should be introduced gradually after pelvic floor conditioning. A physiotherapist specialising in pelvic floor rehabilitation can design a return-to-exercise programme. Avoid Valsalva manoeuvre-heavy exercises (heavy deadlifts, sit-ups with breath-holding) until levator ani strength is established. Post-surgical patients typically return to light activity at 4–6 weeks and full exercise at 3 months.
dailyManagement: Perform at least 3 sets of 10 pelvic floor contractions (holding each for 8–10 seconds) daily.
Follow a regular toileting schedule and never rush or force a bowel movement.
Check the pessary is comfortable and in place if one is being used; attend routine clinic changes every 3–6 months.
Track symptom changes in a simple diary to share with the clinical team at review.
Report any new bleeding, increasing prolapse, urinary difficulties, or pain promptly.
prognosis: The natural history of rectocele varies widely: many Grade 1–2 defects remain stable for years, particularly with consistent conservative management. Without treatment, larger rectoceles tend to progress slowly, worsening with menopause, weight gain, or further straining. Conservative management with PFMT and bowel optimisation achieves meaningful symptom relief in approximately 40–60% of women with Grade 1–2 rectocele, avoiding surgery entirely. After posterior colporrhaphy, anatomical success rates exceed 80% at one year, and the majority of women with a dominant bulge symptom are satisfied with the outcome. Evacuation symptoms are less reliably resolved by surgery — up to 30% of women note unchanged or worsened bowel symptoms postoperatively if the defaecatory dysfunction has a neuromuscular rather than purely structural basis. Recurrence rates after primary repair are approximately 5–15% at five years, rising with persistent straining, obesity, or inadequate fascial tissue quality.
recoveryTimeline: After posterior colporrhaphy, most women are discharged within 24–48 hours. Perineal discomfort and swelling settle over 2–4 weeks. Light non-strenuous activity resumes at 2 weeks, driving at 2–3 weeks, and sexual intercourse at 6–8 weeks. Heavy lifting and high-impact exercise are avoided for 3 months to allow fascial healing. Full wound healing is usually complete by 6–8 weeks. Follow-up examination at 6–8 weeks and then at 6–12 months assesses anatomical result and functional outcomes.
complications: Recurrence of prolapse — anatomical recurrence in 5–15% at five years, more common when underlying risk factors such as straining or obesity persist.
De novo or worsened dyspareunia — reported by 10–20% of women after posterior colporrhaphy, particularly after overzealous narrowing of the vaginal calibre.
Persistent obstructed defecation — surgery corrects the structural bulge but may not resolve dyssynergic defecation if there is a neuromuscular component.
Wound dehiscence or infection — superficial wound breakdown occurs in 2–5% of cases and usually heals with local wound care.
Progression to complete pelvic organ prolapse — untreated or recurrent rectocele may be accompanied by worsening of anterior and apical compartments over time.
Fecal incontinence — rarely caused by the rectocele itself but may be unmasked by repair if the anal sphincter was previously supported by the prolapsed tissue.

supportResources.name: AUGS — American Urogynecologic Society patient resources
supportResources.description: Patient education materials on pelvic organ prolapse, treatment options, and pelvic floor health from the leading U.S. subspecialty society.

supportResources.name: NHS — Pelvic organ prolapse overview
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS guide to pelvic organ prolapse types, symptoms, and treatment including physiotherapy and surgery.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Pelvic Floor Disorders
supportResources.description: U.S. National Institute overview of pelvic floor disorders including prolapse, incontinence, and treatment approaches.

sources.title: Barber MD, Maher C. Epidemiology and outcome assessment of pelvic organ prolapse. Int Urogynecol J, 2013
Hagen S, Stark D. Conservative prevention and management of pelvic organ prolapse in women. Cochrane Database Syst Rev, 2011
Maher C et al. Surgical management of pelvic organ prolapse in women. Cochrane Database Syst Rev, 2013
ACOG Practice Bulletin 214 — Pelvic Organ Prolapse, 2019
Jelovsek JE et al. Bowel symptoms 1 year after surgery for anterior vaginal wall prolapse. Am J Obstet Gynecol, 2008
AUGS/SUFU Pelvic Organ Prolapse FAQ Pamphlet

confusedWithConditions.name: Enterocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An enterocele is a herniation of the small bowel through the upper posterior vaginal wall, producing an apical rather than lower posterior bulge; rectocele involves the rectum bulging through the lower posterior wall.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cystocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A cystocele is a bulge of the bladder through the anterior vaginal wall, causing urinary rather than bowel symptoms; rectocele affects the posterior wall and causes defaecatory difficulty.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rectal prolapse
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rectal prolapse involves full-thickness protrusion of the rectum through the anus as a red, cylindrical mass; rectocele is an internal bulge of the rectal wall into the vaginal canal without rectal eversion.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anal prolapse
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anal prolapse is a protrusion of the anal lining through the anal opening, visible externally and diagnosed anally; rectocele presents as a vaginal bulge detected on gynaecological examination.

relatedConditions.name: Enterocele
relatedConditions.relevance: Enterocele and rectocele frequently coexist in the posterior vaginal compartment and are often repaired together, particularly when both a rectal and small-bowel component are found at defaecography.

relatedConditions.name: Cystocele
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common prolapse companion to rectocele; combined anterior and posterior repair addresses both compartments in one operation, reducing total symptom burden.

relatedConditions.name: Pelvic muscle wasting
relatedConditions.relevance: Levator ani atrophy and pelvic muscle weakness are the underlying mechanisms common to rectocele and other pelvic floor disorders, and pelvic floor training targets this shared pathology.

relatedConditions.name: Perineocele
relatedConditions.relevance: Perineocele — herniation through the perineal body — is closely related to rectocele and the two are often distinguished only at defaecography or surgical exploration.

relatedConditions.name: Endometriosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Endometriosis of the rectovaginal septum can mimic or worsen rectocele symptoms and should be excluded when posterior vaginal pain or bowel symptoms are prominent.

faqs.question: What does a rectocele feel like?
faqs.answer: Most women describe a soft bulge or lump at the vaginal opening, pelvic heaviness that worsens through the day, and a sense that stool is not fully expelled after a bowel movement. Some notice they need to press upward on the back of the vaginal wall with a finger to empty the bowel completely.

faqs.question: Is a rectocele dangerous?
faqs.answer: A rectocele is not life-threatening. It is a structural weakening of the vaginal wall rather than a cancer or infection. However, larger defects can significantly impair bowel function and quality of life, and rare complications such as mucosal ulceration or urinary obstruction require prompt treatment.

faqs.question: Can a rectocele heal on its own?
faqs.answer: Small Grade 1 rectoceles frequently remain stable for years and may not worsen, but fascial defects do not fully close without treatment. Conservative measures such as pelvic floor exercises and bowel management can substantially reduce symptoms and prevent progression, though they do not repair the underlying structural defect.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for a rectocele?
faqs.answer: Surgery is not needed for most rectoceles. Grade 1–2 defects are managed conservatively with pelvic floor exercises, bowel optimisation, and a vaginal pessary. Surgery is considered when Grade 2–3 prolapse causes significant symptoms that have not responded to at least 3–6 months of conservative treatment, or when quality of life is substantially affected.

faqs.question: How successful is posterior colporrhaphy?
faqs.answer: Posterior colporrhaphy corrects the vaginal bulge in 76–96% of women at one year. Bowel symptoms such as incomplete emptying improve in approximately 60–75% of women. Recurrence over five years occurs in roughly 5–15% of cases, more often when straining or obesity persists.

faqs.question: Can I have sex with a rectocele?
faqs.answer: Many women with rectocele continue to have comfortable sexual intercourse, particularly with small-to-moderate defects. Larger rectoceles may cause dyspareunia from the posterior wall distortion. After surgical repair, intercourse is usually avoided for 6–8 weeks to allow full vaginal healing.

faqs.question: What exercises help a rectocele?
faqs.answer: Pelvic floor muscle training — Kegel exercises — is the most evidence-based conservative intervention. A physiotherapist specialising in urogynaecology can teach correct technique and provide a structured programme. Low-impact exercise such as walking and swimming is beneficial; high-impact activity should be introduced gradually after pelvic floor conditioning.

faqs.question: What causes a rectocele?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is tearing or stretching of the rectovaginal fascia during vaginal childbirth, particularly after a prolonged second stage, instrumental delivery, or large baby. Ageing, postmenopausal oestrogen decline, chronic constipation and straining, obesity, and prior hysterectomy all contribute to weakening of the posterior vaginal support.

faqs.question: Is splinting to empty the bowel normal?
faqs.answer: Splinting — pressing upward on the back of the vaginal wall or perineum to complete defecation — is a recognised symptom of moderate-to-large rectocele reported by 30–75% of affected women. It is a useful temporary self-management technique but signals that a clinical assessment for treatment options is warranted.

faqs.question: Can a pessary treat a rectocele?
faqs.answer: A vaginal pessary can mechanically support the posterior vaginal wall and significantly reduce prolapse symptoms without surgery. Pessaries require fitting by a clinician, regular cleaning every 3–6 months, and monitoring for vaginal erosion. They are a good option for women who prefer to avoid surgery or have significant medical comorbidities.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a rectocele and an enterocele?
faqs.answer: A rectocele is a bulge of the front wall of the rectum into the lower posterior vaginal canal. An enterocele is a herniation of the small bowel and peritoneum through the upper posterior vaginal wall near the vault. Both cause a vaginal bulge, but they occur at different levels and are distinguished by examination, defaecography, or pelvic MRI.

faqs.question: How is a rectocele diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by pelvic examination: the clinician uses a speculum to assess the posterior vaginal wall at rest and during Valsalva, and grades it using the POP-Q system. Defaecography or dynamic MRI is added when functional bowel obstruction or a co-existing rectal or small-bowel component needs to be clarified.

faqs.question: Will losing weight help my rectocele?
faqs.answer: Weight loss can meaningfully reduce pelvic floor pressure and symptom severity. A 10–15% reduction in body weight reduces prolapse symptoms in overweight and obese women and may slow progression. It does not repair the fascial defect but is a valuable part of conservative management.

faqs.question: Can rectocele cause urinary symptoms?
faqs.answer: Rectocele primarily causes bowel symptoms, but large posterior prolapse can exert pressure on the bladder base and contribute to urinary urgency or frequency. Many women with rectocele have concurrent anterior prolapse or stress incontinence, which are separate structural defects that warrant independent assessment.

faqs.question: What happens if rectocele is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Small rectoceles often remain stable for years. Larger defects may gradually worsen with menopause, weight gain, or continued straining. Without treatment, bowel emptying difficulties and pelvic pressure can progress, and the prolapse may extend beyond the vaginal opening. Rare but serious complications include mucosal ulceration and urinary obstruction from concurrent severe prolapse.

faqs.question: Can rectocele cause constipation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The rectal pocket created by the rectocele traps stool, making complete evacuation difficult. This functional obstruction can cause or worsen constipation. Addressing the structural defect — whether conservatively or surgically — often improves bowel emptying, but a concurrent dietary and bowel rehabilitation programme is essential for the best outcome.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after rectocele surgery?
faqs.answer: Most women are discharged within 24–48 hours. Perineal discomfort settles over 2–4 weeks. Light activity resumes at 2 weeks, driving at 2–3 weeks, and sexual intercourse at 6–8 weeks. Heavy lifting and high-impact exercise are avoided for 3 months to allow complete fascial healing.

faqs.question: What grade of rectocele needs surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is typically recommended for Grade 2–3 rectocele that causes significant symptoms — particularly splinting, incomplete emptying, or a visible external bulge — after conservative measures have been tried for at least 3–6 months. Grade 1 and mildly symptomatic Grade 2 rectoceles are managed non-surgically.

faqs.question: Does childbirth always cause a rectocele?
faqs.answer: Not always. Most vaginal deliveries cause some degree of rectovaginal fascial stretching, but a clinically significant rectocele depends on the severity of the birth-related trauma, subsequent healing, and later risk factors such as menopause and obesity. Nulliparous women can also develop rectocele through connective-tissue weakness or chronic straining.

faqs.question: Is rectocele the same as pelvic organ prolapse?
faqs.answer: Rectocele is one type of pelvic organ prolapse — specifically a posterior-compartment prolapse involving the rectum. Pelvic organ prolapse is the broader term covering all compartments: anterior (cystocele), posterior (rectocele, enterocele), and apical (uterine or vault prolapse). Many women have prolapse in more than one compartment simultaneously.

metaDescription: A rectocele bulges the rectal wall into the vagina, causing pelvic pressure and incomplete bowel emptying. Learn symptoms, exercises and repair options.
